critic_raw:
This is a carefully designed study. The temporal censoring is strict and explicit, the patient-level split prevents repeat-encounter leakage, the exclusion criteria are clinically motivated, and the authors correctly scope their claim (discriminative improvement, not causal benefit). Let me work through the areas where genuine methodological risk exists.

**1. Temporal confounding via test-ordering behavior as a feature**

The methodology explicitly retains missingness indicators because 'test-ordering behavior is itself informative.' This is epidemiologically true but introduces a subtle form of label leakage that is not resolved by the 6-hour censoring rule. A physician who suspects admission will order a broader panel of tests — chest X-ray, troponin series, urine culture — within the first 6 hours precisely because they anticipate admission. The pattern of which tests were ordered, not just their results, encodes the physician's implicit admission decision before the formal disposition is recorded. This is not a future-data leak (all orders are pre-6-hour), but it is a proxy-label signal: the model learns to predict admission partly by recognizing the signature of a physician who has already decided to admit. The claim being made is that test-ordering patterns reflect early clinical state; the alternative is that they reflect physician intent, which is a downstream correlate of the outcome rather than an independent physiological signal. The consequence is that the multimodal model's AUROC advantage over structured baselines may partly reflect its superior ability to exploit this proxy signal rather than superior extraction of physiological content from text. This matters because in deployment, a model that relies on order-pattern proxies will degrade whenever ordering protocols change (e.g., ED fast-track protocols, sepsis bundles, care pathway standardization). Evidence would come from an ablation that removes all order-presence indicators and retains only resulted values — if AUROC drops substantially for the proposed model but not the baseline, proxy-label exploitation is occurring.

**2. The baseline is not the right comparator for the specific hypothesis**

The hypothesis is that joint multimodal encoding of text and physiological trajectory improves over structured variables alone. The baseline is GBT on structured variables excluding free text. This comparison conflates two distinct contributions: (a) the information content of free text, and (b) the temporal sequence modeling of vitals/labs. A GBT on structured features cannot model the trajectory of vitals as an ordered sequence, so a performance gap could be attributable entirely to temporal encoding rather than to the text modality. To isolate the text contribution — which is the novel claim of a 'multimodal' system — the methodology needs a text-ablated version of the transformer (same architecture, same temporal encoding, no text input) as an additional baseline. Without it, the headline result ('multimodal transformer beats GBT') does not establish that text adds value; it only establishes that the full system adds value over a structurally simpler baseline. The missing baseline is a structured-only transformer, not a text-only addition. Evidence: run the proposed model with text encoders zeroed out or replaced with a learned null token and report the AUROC delta.

**3. Hospital-stratified results are reported but the split design conflates case-mix preservation with site-level generalization**

The stratified split preserves each site's admission prevalence in each partition by design — this is stated as intentional. But this means the test set is drawn from the same temporal window as the training set across all sites. There is no out-of-time or out-of-site holdout. The stated objective is comparison of multimodal vs. structured signal, not generalization, and the authors acknowledge this. However, the hospital-stratified results are presented as if they characterize site-to-site robustness. They do not: a model that has seen training data from every hospital, stratified to match each hospital's prevalence, will perform well on that hospital's test slice not because it generalizes but because it was trained on similar data from the same site. The claim being made is that hospital-stratified results characterize robustness; the actual guarantee is only that the comparison is fair within each site's slice. This is not a fatal flaw for the primary hypothesis, but it means the hospital-stratified figures cannot be interpreted as leave-one-site-out generalization evidence. Evidence: a single held-out hospital (smallest site acceptable for stable AUROC estimation) evaluated with a model never trained on that site's data would separate these interpretations.

**4. Prerequisite assumption: the transformer can learn useful representations from short, institutionally stereotyped clinical text**

The multimodal transformer's advantage depends on the triage note containing signal not captured by structured fields. Triage notes in ED settings are frequently templated, brief (median length often under 50 tokens at many sites), and highly formulaic ('Patient presents with chest pain, onset 2 hours ago, rated 7/10'). The model's note-length subgroup analysis is a partial check, but length is a proxy for information density only if longer notes are also more semantically variable. If notes are institutionally stereotyped — the same phrases recurring across thousands of encounters — the text encoder will learn to pattern-match institutional boilerplate rather than extract clinical nuance, and its contribution over a bag-of-words feature in the GBT baseline becomes unclear. The prerequisite is that triage notes contain non-redundant information relative to the structured fields. This can be examined empirically: compute the mutual information between note embeddings and admission outcome after conditioning on structured features, or compare the full multimodal model against a GBT augmented with TF-IDF or simple keyword features from the same notes. If the keyword-augmented GBT closes most of the gap, the transformer text encoder is not doing sophisticated linguistic reasoning.

**Summary assessment:** Issues 1 and 2 are the most consequential. Issue 1 (order-pattern proxy signal) could inflate the apparent advantage of both models but especially the transformer, which is better positioned to exploit the joint pattern of order sequences and text. Issue 2 (missing ablation baseline) means the headline claim — that multimodal text+trajectory modeling outperforms structured baselines — cannot be cleanly attributed to the text modality. Issues 3 and 4 are real but bounded: issue 3 is a scope/interpretation problem rather than a validity problem, and issue 4 is empirically checkable within the described experimental framework. The methodology is otherwise rigorous. The verdict reflects that issues 1 and 2 are genuine structural gaps that the study as described cannot resolve.
defender_raw:
## Pass 1 — Analysis

### 1. Temporal Leakage

**What a critic would claim:** The 6-hour prediction time window is porous. Features like 'early lab-order indicators' could carry implicit information about the clinician's gestalt toward admission, and triage notes may contain forward-looking statements ('plan to admit,' 'awaiting bed'). This constitutes soft leakage that inflates the multimodal model's apparent advantage over structured-only baselines.

**Assessment:** The design addresses hard leakage explicitly and credibly — bed requests, consultant recommendations, post-6-hour orders, and post-time text are excluded by name. The triage note inclusion is defensible: triage notes are written at or near arrival and represent prospectively documented clinical reasoning, not retrospective annotation. However, the critic's concern about naturalistic soft leakage in free text is legitimate. Triage notes in real systems sometimes contain phrases like 'likely admission' written by experienced nurses based on rapid gestalt. This would advantage the text encoder in a way that does not reflect the pure signal the study claims to isolate. The methodology does not describe a text-cleaning step or a sensitivity analysis that removes obviously forward-looking phrases. This is a real gap, though it is also an inherent feature of any study that uses clinical notes honestly — sanitizing notes aggressively introduces its own distortion. The appropriate response is to include a sensitivity analysis omitting the triage note from the text encoder and report whether the AUROC delta shrinks substantially. The design does not explicitly commit to this. Verdict on this point: **partial concession** — the design is aware of temporal discipline but has a non-trivial blind spot on naturalistic forward leakage in free text.

### 2. Patient-Level vs. Encounter-Level Split

**What a critic would claim:** Assigning all encounters from the same patient to a single partition is described as preventing repeat-patient leakage, but this also means the patient population distributions could differ across partitions in ways not fully corrected by stratification on hospital and admission status. Patients who are frequent ED visitors may dominate training and be absent from test, or vice versa.

**Assessment:** This is a genuine design tension but not a fatal flaw. The patient-level assignment is the correct choice for avoiding leakage — allowing the same patient's demographics and clinical patterns to appear in both training and test would produce an optimistic bias. The stratification by hospital and admission status preserves the most operationally important marginal distributions. The concern about frequent-flier patients is real but scoped: it means the test set may have a slightly different distribution of patient complexity than a random encounter-level split. This would produce a conservative bias (harder test), not an optimistic one, because frequent visitors with known clinical histories would be concentrated in training. This is acceptable and arguably the more clinically honest evaluation. The methodology is sound here.

### 3. Multimodal Transformer Complexity vs. Baseline Calibration

**What a critic would claim:** 'Tuned with comparable effort' is not a well-defined constraint. The multimodal transformer has vastly more hyperparameters and architectural choices than gradient-boosted trees. In practice, 'comparable effort' almost certainly means the GBT is more thoroughly explored relative to its effective search space, or that the transformer is under-tuned. Either direction is problematic for a fair comparison.

**Assessment:** This is a valid criticism. The methodology acknowledges comparable tuning effort but does not operationalize it — no mention of compute budget parity, number of trials, or whether Bayesian optimization vs. grid search was used for each. In practice, most practitioners spend more wall-clock time on GBT due to faster iteration cycles, which means the transformer may be under-explored. The correct response is to report validation learning curves and hyperparameter sensitivity analyses, and to be explicit that the transformer result is a lower bound on what a more exhaustively tuned model could achieve. The study scope is appropriate — this is a discrimination comparison, not a production deployment optimization — but the incomparability of 'effort' is a real confound. This needs more operational specificity in the methods section. **Partial concession.**

### 4. Temporal Generalizability — No Time-Based Split

**What a critic would claim:** A random encounter-level split (even with patient-level grouping) does not test the model's performance on future data. Admission patterns, coding practices, and triage note styles shift over time. A temporally stratified or forward-chaining split is necessary to claim operational validity.

**Assessment:** The design explicitly acknowledges this: 'the objective is to compare how much additional early clinical signal the multimodal model extracts, not to measure adaptation to a changing policy regime.' This is a stated scope limitation, not an oversight. The study is measuring discriminative signal extraction under a shared data-generating process, which is a legitimate scientific question. The design does not claim the model will generalize to future years. The critic's point is valid for deployment studies; for this study's stated purpose, the random split is appropriate. **Defense holds.** However, the reported AUROC values should not be used in grant applications or procurement arguments as evidence of future performance without explicit caveats — the paper should reinforce this scope boundary strongly.

### 5. Outcome Definition — Administrative Disposition

**What a critic would claim:** Admission within 24 hours taken from administrative records may conflate true clinical admissions with observation status, hold-for-results decisions that never formalize, or patients who were admitted due to boarding issues rather than medical necessity. This outcome heterogeneity means the model is predicting a mixture of clinically distinct events.

**Assessment:** The 24-hour window with administrative plus admission-order confirmation is a reasonable proxy for 'patient needs inpatient-level care.' The exclusion of direct transfers already assigned inpatient care is a notable strength. The observation status concern is real — CMS's 2-midnight rule creates ambiguity between observation and inpatient admission that varies by hospital and over time. If the system does not have clean observation vs. inpatient flags, the outcome is noisier than it appears. The design does not address this. However, for a bed management decision support tool (the stated operational use), the distinction matters less — from a capacity perspective, a patient occupying an inpatient bed is the relevant event regardless of billing status. The noise introduced by observation status ambiguity would attenuate both models' performance roughly equally, so it should not systematically bias the comparison. **Defense holds for the comparison, partial concession for the outcome's clinical precision.**

### 6. Subgroup Analyses — Multiple Comparisons and Power

**What a critic would claim:** Subgroup checks by acuity, shift, interpreter use, and note length are underpowered for meaningful inference at hospital-stratified level. Without pre-specified hypothesis directions or correction for multiplicity, these become exploratory fishing.

**Assessment:** The design calls these 'subgroup checks,' not formal hypothesis tests — this framing is appropriate. The primary comparison is pooled AUROC, and subgroup analyses are described as characterization, not confirmation. The risk is that reviewers or readers will over-interpret exploratory subgroup differences. The methodology should be explicit that these are hypothesis-generating, which it is implicitly through the word 'checks.' Power calculations are not described, but this is standard for subgroup analyses in this genre of study. **Defense holds with the caveat that the paper must be explicit about the exploratory nature of subgroup findings.**

### 7. Missingness Indicators as Features

**What a critic would claim:** Including missingness indicators for labs/vitals introduces a proxy for clinician ordering behavior, which may reflect site-specific protocols rather than transferable clinical signal. The model may learn that 'troponin not ordered' is informative at one hospital because that hospital only orders troponin for chest-pain presentations — but this ordering behavior encodes case mix, not physiology, and may not transfer.

**Assessment:** The design acknowledges this explicitly: 'test-ordering behavior is itself informative.' This is scientifically defensible — from the bed manager's perspective, a patient who had a troponin ordered within 6 hours is, empirically, more likely to be admitted than one who did not, regardless of the underlying reason. The question of whether this signal transfers across sites is addressed by hospital-stratified reporting. If the multimodal model's advantage over GBT shrinks substantially at certain hospitals, this is interpretable as a site-specific ordering-behavior confound rather than generalizable physiological signal. The design handles this appropriately through stratified evaluation. **Defense holds.**

### 8. Relative-Time Embeddings — Implementation Soundness

**What a critic would claim:** Relative-time embeddings for irregular time series require careful implementation. If the embedding is not truly relative (anchored at prediction time minus event time, not wall-clock time), there is temporal leakage through time-of-day or seasonal patterns that correlate with admission rates via census effects.

**Assessment:** The design mentions 'relative-time embeddings' but does not specify whether these are relative to arrival, to the prediction time, or absolute timestamps. If they are relative to arrival time (which is standard), they encode elapsed time in the ED, which is clean. If they encode wall-clock time of day, they are a proxy for hospital census and boarding pressure — which is informative but also a confound for generalization. This is an implementation detail that the methodology should specify. It is not a fatal flaw but is a genuine gap in the description. **Partial concession — needs specification.**

### 9. Calibration and Deployment Readiness

**What a critic would claim:** Reporting calibration slope/intercept and ECE is appropriate, but if the model is miscalibrated across hospitals (as is common for models trained on pooled multi-site data), the clinical usability claim is undermined even if AUROC is strong.

**Assessment:** The design reports hospital-stratified results, which will surface calibration heterogeneity. The stated operational use is ranking (sorting census by admission likelihood), for which AUROC is the right primary metric — miscalibration does not impair ranking performance, only probability estimation. The paper should distinguish between the ranking use case (where calibration is less critical) and any future probability-based thresholding use case (where it is essential). The design does this by specifying AUROC as primary and reporting calibration as a characterization metric. **Defense holds.**

---

## Summary of Genuine Weaknesses Identified

1. **Naturalistic forward leakage in triage notes** — not fully mitigated; sensitivity analysis needed.
2. **'Comparable tuning effort' is not operationalized** — creates an uncontrolled confound in the main comparison.
3. **Relative-time embedding specification is ambiguous** — could allow wall-clock temporal confounding.
4. **Observation status ambiguity in outcome** — not addressed; acceptable for the comparison but reduces clinical precision.

These weaknesses are real but mostly scoped. Items 1 and 2 are the most consequential for the validity of the primary comparison claim.

## Pass 2 — Verdict Selection

The methodology is largely well-designed for its stated scope. The identified weaknesses are not individually fatal: soft leakage in text is a known limitation of any clinical NLP study using real notes; tuning comparability is an execution gap that attenuates rather than invalidates findings; relative-time embedding ambiguity is a specification gap that is resolvable. None of these rise to the level of 'the main result cannot be trusted.' The stated scope limitations (no temporal generalization claim, no causal deployment claim) are honest and appropriate.

However, the combination of (1) unmitigated naturalistic leakage in the text modality that could inflate the multimodal model's advantage and (2) under-specified tuning comparability creates enough uncertainty that the magnitude of the primary comparison result cannot be trusted without empirical verification of these specific confounds. A sensitivity analysis on the triage note and a reported hyperparameter search protocol would resolve both. Until those are run, the direction of the effect is likely trustworthy but the magnitude is uncertain.

Verdict: **empirical_test_agreed** — the design is sound in structure, but the two key confounds identified require empirical sensitivity analyses to bound their influence on the primary AUROC comparison before conclusions about multimodal advantage can be stated with confidence.
all_issues_raised: Test-ordering patterns within 6 hours encode physician admission intent, creating a proxy-label signal that is temporally clean but conceptually confounded; an ablation removing order-presence indicators is needed to bound this effect.

The primary baseline (GBT on structured features) conflates the contribution of free text with the contribution of temporal sequence modeling; a text-ablated transformer is the missing comparator needed to isolate the text modality's incremental value.

Hospital-stratified test results are presented as robustness evidence but are drawn from the same temporal window as training data at every site; they establish fair within-site comparison, not cross-site generalization.

The transformer's text contribution assumes triage notes contain non-redundant signal beyond structured fields; institutionally stereotyped, short notes may mean the text encoder learns boilerplate patterns that a keyword-augmented baseline could replicate.
all_issues_adjudicated: Test-ordering patterns within 6 hours encode physician admission intent as a proxy-label signal; the defender reframes this as clinically valid and handled by stratified evaluation, but does not commit to the ablation study needed to bound the proxy-label effect, leaving the magnitude of inflation unresolved.

The primary baseline conflates text contribution with temporal sequence modeling contribution; the defender does not address this issue directly in Pass 2 and offers no commitment to a text-ablated transformer baseline, leaving the attribution of the multimodal advantage unestablished.

Hospital-stratified results cannot be interpreted as cross-site generalization evidence because training data from every site is used; the defender concedes this is a scope/interpretation issue but does not propose a remedy, so the over-interpretation risk survives.

Triage note stereotypy may mean text encoder learns boilerplate rather than extracting non-redundant clinical signal; the defender does not directly rebut this in Pass 2 and no commitment to a keyword-augmented baseline comparison is made.
